speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dick Benge, his wife Ella Mae Benge, and their old friend Art Farley trade stories about dances, chivalries, chicken thieving, and other amusing trickery, and about some serious grudges and fights. Dick also explains water witching and discusses the effects of farming and logging on the country.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: It was how the country used to look before they logged it. It was beautiful. Beautiful country. You know the fire went through here in 1910. When I come here in 13, we could drive out and buggy, practically over any of these hills. No brush. Just through the big timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that what the fire did to you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: That's what the fire did to you. Killed all the underbrush and everything. They did that big red fur and white fur and yellow pine and salmon. Yeah, it was beautiful. You go out here deer hunting, you see a deer a quarter mile or sometimes a half mile. Right out through the trees. Now that is when it's nice. So, they cut the big stuff off, you know, and then they started going up the brush. Been going to brush ever since. I don't know how they'd top it. I just don't know how they would go about it. Well, I think before service get out of it and thin it out. They're going to have to if they ever have much more timber. It'll naturally nature will thin it, but it takes so long. Takes so long for nature to thin it, you know, for the large trees to get rotten out and decay. And the best of them live through it. But, if you just let it go, boy, you can go up there places right now where there's no brush and a log there. And, no, you can't either walk through it. Let's go down. Little trees like that, some of them big as a cub.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't watch the Forest Service fans, but they don't seem to be doing much about it. There isn't many of them in here. Forest Service boys.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Just a few.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of brush is it that's growing up now, mostly?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, white fur, red fur, prince fur, and black pine. And there's lots of chaparral brush in here. And buck brush.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: And, well, any kind of brush you want to name that's in there. Right out in the timber now. Just taking over. When we ever get a part in here and clean it off, we won't have nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean it'll just burn all the timber too?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: It'll burn everything now. Because before, you know, that big yellow pine was the big, large... Of course, it burnt it at the stumps. It scarred it quite a bit at some stumps. But it never fell any of it. Never burned any of it down. And that's one thing I like. You can look right over there on that hill now and see how bare that is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you can see that brush through there, see?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: All that green stuff is brush. Chaparral, buck brush, service. All such as that and no timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that stuff just shoot up as soon as they cut the timber down?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, just quickly it gets light and it just goes right up. It's kind of a second dose, you know. That's what it takes over next.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if you let this stuff go long enough, would it get to be big timber like the other stuff you've been over?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, here's the idea, you know. It's going to take several years for that to happen. It'll take a couple of generations for that to get around. Because we cut lots of trees in here and skidded them out when I was three, four hundred years old. Well, just how long it's going to take them to get back to that size again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: None of us will ever see it, will we?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: But if they thin it out, that'll go a lot faster. I was heading to the other line up here. I took the dose and went right through and got all the small stuff out. Went and rolled it up and left all the stuff that big. Some of it like that at the ground. Well, and here last two years ago, I took off three hundred and some thousand where I did that. Let it go just a few years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is your hammer? Which one?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Any hammer!

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't know, I think they're going upstairs. No, I took it to the shop. I'll take it back. It's out in the shop, man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any upstairs at all?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, they've got kids' hammer up there if you want to hit. That's one you told was up there at Neil to use, so I didn't use it. I don't think he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is the brush that's so big now, was that brush there before the timber was cut? The same brush but a small house? Or is it all new brush and smooth?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, that's the second growth, you see, after the timber's all out. Nature. Well, you know, they used to pasture all this whole country. Lamb from, well, he had a place down below Potlatch and then he had a place down there at Hay, Washington, down on the river. He had about five hundred and eighty cattle in here. Well, you could go see them cattle any place you wanted to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you take, East Berlin? What?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: So, cattle could eat sheep, train the dog through here. No, I don't suppose sheep can get through here at all. No, it's getting the brush, the underbrush getting so thick that cattle can't get through it. Well, back in the old days, this country was used more for cattle than it was for growing crops. Oh, yeah, well, this is all timber, none of it, you know. Well, down here about two mile and a half was the closest place there was up here with any farmland at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Till when?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Till, oh, I moved up here in the fall of 25. And then we started cleaning up and bought it off the company, you see, this land. So you can see what happened now. Well, I got the dozers in here now. Well, don't take none of them and clean it. But we used to blow the big stuff, you know, dynamite, burn them. You told me. Yeah, Betsy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then when you got it cleared, though, did you use it for grazing or for growing mostly?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, I used it all for growing, grain. So once it was clear, it went into growing. Once it was growing, yes. Good land after you get cleared. Yeah. All that pine ground is good, good land. That is the yellow pine, but not the white pine and black pine. But the yellow pine, in the face it grows, it's good ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would it go up in these places in here when you caught it and got tailed? Then what? Then what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: When you caught it and got tailed. When you caught it and got tailed.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the weather has changed a lot in this country since you first came?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: That it's changed a lot. You know, we first come here now 13 in the spring of 14. We started here next morning after Christmas, sleigh -hauling, putting 90 days on a sleigh, hauling logs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if you go out now, if you could get 10 days for sleigh -hauling for logs, you'd be doing good, wouldn't you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Takes lots of snow for a sleigh for logs, you know. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Yeah,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: it's, I don't know what the country is going to come to you. I don't know. You've seen a lot more of it than I have, so maybe you had a better idea than I do. Well, I don't know. I just, I can't figure the way it's, I'll tell you right now what they're doing with these tractors and this fertilizing one thing and the other. They're putting on it. They're killing the ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, just killed it. All right. You know, according to the Bible, you were supposed to rest your ground every seven years. Well, you stay in here, you know, and only raise a crop every other year. See, some of all, half of it, one year. That fall, they'd put it in wheat or wheat and put part of it in barley and oats next spring. Just planted every other year, but now they use fertilizer and farm it all. So, I don't go for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does that do to the ground? Huh? What does that do to the ground?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Raise it out so it can't go in. It just, all right. There was Jap down there at the mill. He had a bunch of ground. He raised gardens. We'd puddle out the whole time. So, we was decking logs and the marin couldn't keep up, so they sent up to Bowell and got a half track come down. Well, a half track is, they've got tracks behind, like cat and rubber -wheeled in front. And they're heavy. Well, they'd pull three and four sleds with them, see. And everyone went down and we wondered how we'd... The Jap, the second year after the war, he left. Well, this is the next spring. It hadn't been farmed a year before. Well, we figured he'd farmed it for years and he raised his crop on it. We figured we couldn't get across that flat at all with that rig. The water was out deep in places. We drove that across there and never made a truck in it. Just like driving it on cement. And they've never been able to plow it or get any crop out of it since. They tried it here three years ago. Hard to cut and plow it. You can't all just run together like C -men. So, I don't go for what they're doing. It might be all right. They're making the money now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how long it's gonna last?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I just can't figure it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's gonna take the richness out of the soil?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: It does.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: We'll take a human body. You can give him enough medicine to kill him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can't you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Just take his life. That's all they're doing to the ground. Just taking their life right out of it just to get a crop. Well, boy, the crops they do raise in a hundred, a hundred and ten bushel of wheat there. It's all right, I guess, but the crop, the ground can't stand that. In my notion, maybe I'm wrong. I make lots of mistakes, but I can't see where they're gonna get by with it. No, I wonder too. You know, I'll tell you. You see lots of these hills right now. You just stop and look them over. And look how yellow it is and there's not much grain on them. That's where they've been fertilizing heavy the last ten, twelve years. There's nothing on some of them this year. I noticed. We got some of it right between here and Potlatch. There's nothing much of a dollar. Not enough coal to beat ten bushels of the earth on it. Well, they've pulled all the shrinks out of the ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did it used to be like before they started using the fertilizer as compared to this?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, they used to get a good crop. That is, no, they didn't get a good crop. They would get, well, over around Joel, they were all wheat over there three years off of one foot of cliff ocean. And he got sixty -two bushels an acre on average the three years of wheat. And didn't put no fertilizer on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Only done with summer pollen. Plowed half of it one year and half the next. Yeah. And you could get out through that ground now, then. And you take out through where he plowed it and I'll tell the man you could get through there as an empty truck. Just that looks. You go across the ground now, you can take any wheat field. You can go right out across the truck anyway.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I guess they would say, the people that believe in it, that you can just keep putting more and more fertilizer on it. As long as you put fertilizer on it, the ground will keep producing. But they've got to keep doing it. I don't think they believe they can stop. Well, here's the idea right now. It's cost an important dollar an acre for fertilizer. Well, I can't see why they're gaining much. They have to put that much medicine on it to get that much out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Not in the long run. No.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I'd want to. I make lots of mistakes and maybe I've made one there. But I'm getting that in my head. Well, I'll read on the farm in Nebraska and I'll tell you what. Farm there every year. Over and over. Over and over. Round and round and round. Well, as well, that ground right there, right now, they turn back over to rattlesnakes and jackrabbits and ground squirrels. Farm that every year. Of course, they didn't use. All they used there was manure for fertilizer. We didn't know what fertilizer was then. But, I know we, one year we moved on the place there that we hauled manure there for six weeks. Four horses. Boy, we raised a crop. That's all we put on it. We raised a crop there for several years right on that same ground. But the ground never got hard. It was moist. Loose. And we hauled all the old strawtack bottoms out and spread that on the ground. Strawtack bottoms. Boy, we thrashed, you see. There, you know, they didn't have combines. They all used stationery. Downed the grain and bundled them. And set the machine and held all the grain into it. Well, that left a big show pile there. Well, they let the stock run to it a couple years and tumbled it down and then we hauled out what was on the ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you think straw and manure was better?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Like, yeah. That keeps you ground loose. You know, it keeps you ground loose.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: But, I don't know. It don't seem like... Let's put it this way. Lots of people looking out for themselves today and don't look for their generations coming up. My way of looking at it. Of course, they keep that up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What will be for the younger generations?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Why are they going to take a hold and go ahead and... There won't be nothing left for them to do. Well, there's no ground upon them. That's the way I look at it. Maybe I'm wrong. No, it sounds right to me. I was grieving it. I wonder why it's that way, though. I wonder why it's that way. Money. These fellers is renting this ground and paying high rent each for it. And they're souping the ground to get the... They don't care what they do with the ground. They're paying cash rent. When they get the paying cash rent, they're done with it. They don't care. So, they're just feathering that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the growth? The generations are coming on, why are they going to have nothing?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: The way I am, I make lots of mistakes. That might be one of them, but I don't think there'll be much left for them to... You know, go ahead and farm.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, I wonder.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It doesn't seem like there's much they can do about it, does there? The way they're going, does there to you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: No. Well, I'll tell you what's doing lots of it. See, big co -op company is doing it. We got run right down here. I doubt it if he could tell us just how much land he's a farmer. He's a farmer.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: And, like that, well, he's souping the ground up and boy, he's getting the crop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, how long is it going to last?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: As you say, I think they're putting on a little more each year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: They keep on, keep farming that one piece of ground, and it's going to cost them more than they'll make out of it directly to buy the stuff to put on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think the weather's changed? Do you have any idea on that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, yeah, the weather, well, now, when we come here, it's frosted every month in a year. Well, yeah. That's right. He ain't joking about it. You couldn't plant very few places around here you could plant spuds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, where is this timber being cut off? Let the frost protect the wind and let it come in and keeps it dry or what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't know. I've often thought about that. Could be. But frost, oh boy. See, it used to be a frost. Well, here it used to be, you know, in the early days. We'd go and have four or five feet of snow here on the road and go to Moscow. Wouldn't be any. Now it's the other way around. There's most known Moscow, and it's right here right now. In the wintertime. Yeah. Now, what's called it, I don't know. There's a lot more snow around Troy than there is around here, too. Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah. So.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You haven't heard other people say anything about why they think it, what they think caused it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, I tried to talk here a few years back that what changed it was a Japanese current changed. So. And, you know, these airplanes flying back and forth, they can get in that gap current. They can make a good time going east. That's a better time than the kind of coming back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what, those are the right?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I never got that part along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the game now is the way, compared to the way the game used to be?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, boy, real type deer in there, deer in there, you know. When I come here, by, hmm. Now, of course, that game wasn't there. We had quite a bunch of them, 42 or 43. But, uh, used to be, you just go out here. Well, the way it was, why we wanted a deer, we went and got it when I come here. Everybody did. Nobody said much. But now, the game wasn't right here on us. All the time, I wouldn't go out and kill one on a season under no circumstances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: But the game, oh, boy, is like the game. Oh, kinds of deer. Well, you couldn't help it if he'd run into something, broke his neck. Well, no, run into the fence.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You couldn't help it if he'd run into something below his neck?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, you couldn't help it if the deer run into something, broke his neck. Oh, run into the fence. If I'd have found him, I'd have still taken his beard with me. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was a lot more game than there is now?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, yeah. Well, when I first come here, and, uh, we lived down here about two mile and a half,

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I'd come up the road, oh, probably half a mile an hour in the evening, just before sunset, with a rifle, and I can get six or eight peasants, natives, take back. I'd probably see a hundred of them, but I didn't pay any attention to it. I just got what I wanted and went on back. But now I don't know when I've seen a native peasant, and it used to be alive here with chink peasants, but there's none of them anymore. They're treating the wheat, and it's killing the birds.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Now, one day here, I believe it was about a year ago, with a game order, and I think Van Steffer once turned loose, down here about a mile. And I think I've seen two chinks down there last fall, though I've seen. So, I think they're getting that wheat that they're treating, and, I'm not gonna get it on, and, them, they wouldn't slip around and turn the hot water off on us. Boy, that cold water hit you, you wanted to get out of the bath. You had your bath on, you're taking it right down.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: When I worked down at Camp One, before Dick and I was married,

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: the tank that held the water was out in the sun. It was someplace where the sun shone on it. And the bull cook come and told us that if we wanted to come over before the men come in and take a shower, that the water wasn't hit, but that it was warm. And it was just lukewarm, and we used to go every afternoon before we'd come back to work, before the men come in, and take a shower. Just right.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: No, but there, they had a cast iron boiler there, the water hardly ever cooled off. In that. Anyhow, it was just warm. It was all in the house, honey.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: All just warm. A person, if you want a, you know, your perimeter, but you want a shower, you can take in like a box stall outside and a 50 gallon drum that's open at the top and full of water. And then have your shower down below, where you can turn it on, and it sits in the sun all day, and boy, it's just real nice. And go in there and just shower like nobody's business.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, my wife used to do that on the lookout. They used to bring and fill up a big tank for her, and she used to run down on the rocks. Turn the water on, run down, and get a shower.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you mad?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Come back. Hey, now, I wanted to ask you, and go back to where we were sort of leaving off, I wanted to ask you about water witching.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: When you were walking, I wanted to know that. Yeah, well, I don't know. I tried it, it worked for me, but the way they do it, they cut them a willer or a peach, a fork of them. See, they get two forks about that long, and then they leave a snag down below here. Just like a nigger shooter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A nigger shooter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, just like a nigger shooter is, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, now that's where they put rubbers on and shoot rock through a fork. Boy, we had lots of them nice kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A slingshot?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: No, not a slingshot. Now a slingshot goes on a cord, and you hang a rock, you take your slingshot and put it on, and drop your rock in it. And it's got two strings on it. Well, you got one tied around your wrist, and the other one you hold in your hand. And you hold it over your head or this way, whichever way you want to go, and directly let loose that one you got in your hand, and boy, you can throw a rock from here over to that timber. Of course, they won't go very straight, but you can throw one in the very tanky part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you use them for?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, kids just use them, you know. But a witch, well, I'll show you. They just take a fork like this. They catch these two prongs here, and hold this in the hand, and the butt sticks up here. And whenever you walk across water, if you're a good witcher, that'll turn in your hands and fight all you can do. Take the bark right off. I've seen that boy of mine, I've seen him peel bark right off a willow or peach or a knifletree. Just hold it tight, he just grip it so tight, and she would go down and fight all he could do when he struck water. Now, I'll tell you what happened. It was up here on Herman's Place. They got them come over from Moscow and Wichita. And so my boy and Oscar Swager up here, he was a good water witcher. He was up there one Sunday and Herman was telling about it. They come over from Moscow and where they told him to putt the well. So Oscar, he said, well, Johnny, come on. He said, let's go wish it out for him. So we went over, and one of them went on the hill this way and the other, and he went up that way and they come in together. They come in on the same vein.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Oscar said to Herman, he said, how long a pothole do you got?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: And Herman said, I think it's about four feet and a half, maybe five feet. He said, go get it. He went and got it and he went down and on and he went down four feet and struck water. But he was, oh, half a third from here to that pickup from where the fellers from Moscow told him to dig. And Johnny and him said, no, don't dig here, dig here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where's your water?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: That water's gonna come clear out over the top of that hole, being careful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now there's where them two veins cross, see?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where's the kid's top?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Them fellers from Moscow was all right. There was only about ten -twelve feet for them. It wasn't in the right place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Now he wished, the both wished this, well, I got out here. And one of them struck, I think, Oscar struck water at twenty -eight feet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when Johnny come in from over the hill, he said, you'll have to go, I think it was, what was it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Seventy -four feet.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Your coffee's boiling. To water. Just like mad.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: And so I knowed the feller that, well, his wife long before they was married didn't, I knowed the feller that drilled a well for men. So he went down and went down to the first vein and went on down to the lower vein. I told him what they'd done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want some more fresh coffee?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Not me, thanks, I'm fine. So he was, the drill was just sitting there and him and our both sitting there talking. I know God be home Friday evening. And he said, Dick, we got water, we can pump it dry in twenty minutes, wait twenty minutes and the water's up and three feet at the top of the ground. And he said, I can go deep. So he said, I think that's enough. So I told him get, tear it down and get out of here and he said, I want to find out about it before I drill in the morning. He said, I've done it and I've lost all the water. I knowed he was telling the truth because I had a feller working for me here in, he was a well driller, in fact he was an oil driller too. And he drilled all over Montana in that dry country. So the feller had him drilling a well and he come in one day at noon and he said, I got water. He said, I got water up and it was about four feet at the top of the ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, you know, he said, what are you going to do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He said, go on down deeper, maybe he's getting an artesian well now. He said, just let me tell you something. I may drill, he said, not go down four feet, lose every drop of water you've got. He said, I'll tell you what I'll do. I was to get you water. You may pay me for what water for the well now with them and I'll move out. In other words, he said, I might lose your water. He said, well, he paid you, give him the check for it. He went out and they didn't drill three hours and they lost every drop of water he had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was a black well, wasn't it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: That was a blackie, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he come in and he said, well, you ain't got no water in your well, what's the matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, he said, I told you. I said, it happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, can you bring her back?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He said, I have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you do to get her?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He said, you send and get me a carload of crushed rock. I'll pound the whole hole and maybe I can plug her and maybe I can't. I don't know, I've done it. So he put most of that carload to that rock in that well. And finally he brought the water back, sealed her. So he told him, he said, you better get that out of here. He said, before I burn it out. It's not going any deeper. Sure, that's the way it goes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happens when you go too deep? The water all goes down the hole?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, the water, you strike another stronger vein, the water takes off on it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: A dry vein. Now that's what happened in Montana. See that water that he had struck a stronger vein. He went into a stronger vein. Well, the water took down it. Well, then when he put that rock in there, well, it dammed it up, see. And he pounded her in with the weld wheel. And pounded in, pounded in, pounded in, you know. And finally got her sealed up. Water come back up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That happened here? That happen on your place?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: No, it happened in Montana. In that dry country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How down there?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He was down 280 feet.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Well, I got a better story than that. I dug a well, dug it about 100 feet deep and not a drop of water. I don't know where it was, in Nebraska, I think or someplace. And he was so mad. Because there wasn't any water in that well and he said, I'll fix the SOB. And, oh, he just went in the house and got him about ten -six of dynamite and he just let them and threw it down in there. Said, that's one easy way to fill it up. And, boy, the water really cleared the top of the well.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Ha, did it. Yeah. Yeah, let me tell you something. Grandpa said that that was the truth. I don't know. Let me tell you something. That spring down here at the old camp, the first camp they had, water run clear to the creek a year round. Out of that spring. And, of course, the new boys come in there and they was building a ground camp. Art Tracy was his name and Art said, oh, they wanted to dig the well out. And Art said, oh, they just took a box of powder out of it. He buried a box of powder in it and lost all the water. It is back now, quite a little. It's nothing like it used to be. It never will be back. Yeah, he buried a whole box of powder and set it off. Boy, he broke a nice hole out of that. That's all he had was a hole. Ha, ha, ha. And I had to go down and dig a well down next to the creek. Water for the camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where this guy dug up like a place here, and when the guys came together with the sticks, did he get them both at the same time?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, they crossed. That waterbange crossed, you know, here, there. There's one, Johnny and Swater both of them, they go right over the hill. Goes right out across the road over there and right out with that black pine tree. And they've collared the clear over on the hill under the creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they follow along with the willow?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: With the willow, yeah. It'll lead them right along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you ever see anybody witch?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I've never seen it done. No, never seen it done. Well, they just walk along this way and I'll stick a lead them. Go this way or this way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they hold on to the fork?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, just hold the fork. They hold it this way. No, this way, honey. They hold it with their knuckles up, you see. No, with their knuckles up. So your stick can turn your hand. So either this way or that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how is that? Did that work?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't know. If you've asked me a question, I cannot answer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you done it yourself?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, I've done it myself, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will it work for you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, yes, it'll work. I won't say this work's good for me as it does for John or Flader, but I have done it. So, there you go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you suppose Johnny will ever use that talent any? Might?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Might, yeah. Might be any quit's work and he might use it a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, when did you do it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, just playing around, see. Usually, you know, I mean, play around. Well, I watched Oscar and the boy, I'd watch them do it, and then I'd go up where they'd been and see it worked for me. It worked for me all right, yeah. And then when you get to where it's right strong, your stick will go down. When you get clear down, your stick will got to going this way. And every time it goes down, you count. And that's how many feet it is to water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what does it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't know.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: This sounds awful handy for people in the desert, anyhow. I watched that stick when Oscar and Flader and just down there and go that way. Goes up and down. Just up and down. Not very much, but you can count it. And if you just count, that's just how many it's supposed to take every foot.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Do they bark the stick that they use or is it... No, they just leave the bark right on it. I was in there, stripped the bark right off. Well, it's gripped, it's a tight, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they grip it hard, huh?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, they grip it as tight as they can. Especially as it starts turning and then they start gripping it tighter. And then when you get tighter, it goes well, they close your yard of water. And if she starts down on you, you're pretty much right over your water.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, boy. You know, a lot of funny things have happened. I tell you, the older people, they had ways of doing things that they didn't use machinery. Now they got machinery to do a lot of that.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Yeah, a lot of it was old wives' tales, too.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, some of them worked, yeah. A lot of them made good money with your water.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: It just depended on the person.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The person had the talent?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: No, it's something in the body. I don't know where it's electricity. You know, it's human beings got so much electricity in the body.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now where it's electricity or what is it I couldn't tell you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't know. I never had it explained to me and I don't know. But now you take these universities they don't believe in.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Oh, I think they do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: I don't know if they do or not.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, if they do, they sure have messed it up their turbines. No. About 12, 15 feet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, let's say a guy, does a guy, seems like a guy knows if he's got talent or not, though, doesn't he?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, it looked like if he, you know, he'd... If he ever tried it, he could tell. No. Never seen any of it done. Then, uh, watch the puller that has done it that can do it. Then watch him drill a well. Now there's a puller there in Potlatch which is welds. Well, down here at this first trailer house, my grandson lives in there. He give him $50 to get that well out. He went up over the hill and rung that vein right down there. He got an artesian well.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Yeah, a grandson. A grandson lived down here in this first trailer house. Not really very artesian, but then they can't cap it. It just caused an awful lot, or they couldn't cap it last summer, I guess they've got it capped now. But it just caused him an awful lot of trouble. They put just a little cap on it and then it grew out to the side and just run clear across the road. People were hollering because he made a mud puddle and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does it follow the contours of the land very much or does it just work independent of it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Just like going anyplace. Just like I showed you this in there. Comes right over the hill and right just outside the barn. Down across under the creek and over on that hill clear over there. I don't know how much further it got because over there on that hill around the bramble of the river the spring broke out. And this water vein ran right straight to it. So, I don't know, there must be something to it. We got a little...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they hit the water there, did it just get in place here? You said it got down about four feet and it just sprung up?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, I don't know. Now, something...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did Jimmy go? Jimmy went 80 feet, didn't he?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: 70 or 80 feet before you found the artesian well. So, they was drilling right along and pretty soon here comes the water right over the top. So, they put it in their casing and pulled out. And it's been overflowing ever since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say in a hole in this country, I mean, it's not that hard to get water, is it?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: They get lots of good water right up on top of these high hills. Lots of good wells. Oh, some of them now they've gone over there on rock creek and got all kinds of good water but it went down 200 and... I think 248 feet. Where they got water for it.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: The worst trouble of water around all over this country is that it has a settling in it. Rust. Sediment. Sediment, you know. Ours has. You draw some water and let it set overnight and there's some all around the bottom of the... This spider and everything you could do.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, I don't know, it's a rust.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Yeah, it's rust. That's what it is. Little boy. That water in Moscow, wow.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: You know, I turned...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you like that water in Moscow?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Turn the water on up there... I don't care for it. Up there at the house. And it just stumped and they said it's alright. It passes inspection. And I'd come home and turn the pond water on, which I wouldn't have put a spoonful in my mouth for anything. And it smelt just like that water did. Just stinks.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I want to start talking to you about the old dances. That's what we were starting to talk about last time. He didn't get far because the old machine didn't work. You're having a great time. You tell him about something that answers. He's talking to you. Well, I'm thinking more about the mix ups. Stuff around Princeton and that. Yeah, you're gonna watch one another, you know. There's about three different outfits. There's one along the way, one at Princeton and one at Harvard. Well, they traveled in a bunch from each place.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Tell him about the one at Harvard where that guy comes through your legs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where he what?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Where he tried to get down on the... You brought him out and was keeping him out there and he tried to get down on the floor and crawl between your legs to get back in the hall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I took him out of the hall, isn't it? Why'd you do that? Why?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I'll tell you, we wasn't supposed to go today and give us orders not to come up. There was eight of us, one of them was two cars. So we went up anyhow. One of our bunts, he's seen it wasn't going to start so he thought he started. I went to him and told him, look here Johnny, I said, that's not them's target. I pay my attention, pretty soon he's back at it again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He did what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He was back at it again, trying to start it. I said listen, if you don't shut your damn mouth I'm going to take you out of here. He said you can't do it. I said you said too much right now. So I cut him around this leg over here and got my neck, this arm around his neck and brought him right together. He couldn't do nothing, I packed him out through the door. I turned and he said if you would turn me loose I'll give you my next month's check. I said I don't want your check, I didn't carry you out for that, I just carried you out to save trouble.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He got my own right that time. Then I got my face slapped, that was the best. I threw him a girl's door, she talked quite a bit of him. I was out back in the hall and she said, oh boy, my ear just, I can feel that ringing yet. She slapped me. Her dad had me standing right there close to the door to it. Her dad and I were pretty good friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eddie, he's her father, Eddie said why do you do that for us?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He got no business up here in the first place. He said alright, you got as much business as you have here. He said you and your sister get your clothes, I'm taking you home. So we went home and Eddie was back in about 20 minutes. He danced the rest of the night but the girls wouldn't care.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Oh boy. Turn off your, and I'll tell you something that happened down in Whiteburg. Go on, you can tell me, you don't have to worry about it. But we went to the dance and I never was, oh I got over the floor but I never was a very, very, very good dancer. But Velma was. She was just a real little kitten on the floor, I'm telling you. And that was when the shimmy come in. You know, and you take what they call the elephant walk. You know, you come like this, you know, just, up, box up, and then get at the end and then they'd just stand and shimmy. You know, and then whirl around and then go back over here and then they'd stop and shimmy. And boy, she could really shimmy. And then Papa told her, don't you shimmy anymore, I don't like that. So, he just happened to come in the door and here she come down, you know, and then just walked right down there, you know, and then they stopped and shimmied. And Papa just walked right over and got her by the ear and waltzed her right up there. And he said, you might just as well come go with me too, said, I'm taking you home, taking you both home, the shimmying is out in our house. So, we went home.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: That's fine. Wow.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I guess that was a little too... More fun.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this guy that you got a little bit of a tangle with, was he from Princeton?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, him and I were working together down here in the logging camp, we bought lumberjacks. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was it, honey?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Johnny Olsen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Johnny Olsen, he was a sweet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, what was some of the other stuff that happened?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Sounds like that stuff happened. Early off in the dances. Oh, they watched one another, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean watch? What do you mean watch? Did they keep gnawing each other?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well... Several clicks. And sprayed, you know. Well, they'd give them orders, this bunch not to come and that bunch not to come. When they done that, they'd go anyhow. That's part of the task. Yeah. Just wanted to show them they could. I guess.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: That's what... Honoway used to be quite the place for the dance too. Oh, Honoway and Clark.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: They got into it there one night at Honoway and Coyle Lancaster and I. That's when they had the new hall there.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Boy, one feller, he shot off his mouth. One feller, he was all over the house, hit him. That feller, he hit him, he didn't hurt him at all. He turned around and hit the floor manager. Boy, he floored him. So Jack took this weed out, Jack of Wounds. Coyle Lancaster and I followed him right out and when we got out the door, we had a big banister up about that high. Well, we thought we'd stop all the trouble we could, so Coyle and I got our feet to get in the door and the door swung out. And our backs getting that banister and I couldn't open the door on us. Just what I had a poor little boy sitting in there. Just right for her legs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They did try to get out, didn't they?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh boy, I thought, wow, they're going to push that banister off, but they never got the door open. So directly they quit and went on down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jack and I said, well, are they trying to fight down below?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't know, it doesn't matter whether they couldn't get at it or something.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: So... Well, what did it, was it... I started asking this last time. It never did get you answered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did it, what caused the trouble?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, whiskey. Whiskey a lot. You know, one of them fellas would get a drink or two of whiskey and then, oh boy, they were something pretty younger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Something pretty what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, they owned the whole thing, get a drink or two of whiskey, see. Everybody had to look at them and listen at them and that didn't always work, you know. Some fella down there, he ain't going to stand for that. Well, to help it out down there at Onaway that time, when we did that, well, I think there was eight of us, nine of us down there that night in camp. Johnny went along, we got so we didn't take him, the fella and me got into it up there at Harvard. So the floor of a fella running the dances, he sat a man up there doing a week at camp and we was all there and he hung in and he said, Evan, you just sent me up. He said, you fellas, all come down to the dance from now on the rest of winter. He never charged us anything to dance. We ain't just helping to have peace, I mean, that's all the trouble we ever did have. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was Jack Owens much of a hand to fight?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, he thought he was but he couldn't fight.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Jack was stopped. Sounds like you and Boy didn't like to...

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Oh, I've seen him down on his hands and knees and crawled to the house.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, but there's nobody after me.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Dick drink like so many of the other men. He would go on a bender, boy, and he'd just get drunker than a hoot owl. And then maybe not again for six months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, isn't that right?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: I think the last time I saw my husband really drunk was down at Princeton at Peggy's silver wedding anniversary. And they all... the punch was spiked. But then Dick was out in the back room where he was doing the spike and him and his brother -in -law, Herschel, dribbled.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: No, it wasn't Herschel. Herschel got drunk.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: And Herschel got drunk. He wanted to go home but he wouldn't even come out through the middle of the hall. But Dick was just, oh boy, was he higher in the kite and he's just all over the place when he is. And he got out there and there was a whole... they were dancing. And there was a whole bunch of young people there, you know, like you are and younger. Jerry was there.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Just dancing around and Dick got out and he said, I can do that. And he just twisted, you know, and he'd wiggle his arms and he'd clap for them and he'd dance right around them. All those kids had a ball and there was a hump in the floor there and Dick would get to going around too fast and he'd fall down. He said, this floor's got holes in it and he just bounced up like a rubber ball. Oh boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Dick, tell me, what was it like putting on a bender in the old days? What was it like putting on a bender in the old days?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, you know, they did you pretty cool. I hardly ever got to where I was going on the own tower. I might get off a pool, but then you always got home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that be part of the dance pretty much for Wilson's class?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, oh yeah, they did.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, a lot of that fighting was nothing but whiskey that done it. But I can tell you for one thing, it was all the drinking I ever done. I never did get mad but once. Boy, I'd imagine if I'd killed the crew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that like?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, well, he... Hadn't been too long ago. Well, he called me another man and I didn't like him and I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Called you what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Called another neighbor down here. He called me, I come in and he called me...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sam?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Sam Crumley. He said hello, Sam walked up to shake hands with me and I wouldn't shake hands with him. He was already pretty full in. I didn't... I'd all the time never got mad when I was drinking. I understand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do to get you mad?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Well, he called him Sam Crumley. He must, to begin with, he mistaken him for Sam Crumley because he didn't know him very well. And he lived right down here. Oh, I see.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Sam was my cousin and him and I never did get along, couldn't get along, never got along.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Boy, it made me mad. Well, tell me why you didn't get along.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, I'm gonna lay the law right down. When Weecey's mother's place there one night at a dance and my sister, she's... I was 21 and he was about 17 and my sister was there and... He went up and wanted to dance and she said, no I can't. I've got this dance. He would often give her a cussing. Well, I come from there, flicking his hair for him right there and all that, but now listen, boy, he's not of age than I am. And I hadn't met her so I didn't. Well, I never did like him after that. And he done so much more than that afterwards, but not to me. He... I don't know, it doesn't matter why he didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it went on until I was riding with a feller and he was awful good friends with Sam and he said, what do you do if they don't waylay you some night?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: They did. And these boys and the old man and the waylay one just knocked the victims out of here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Out of somebody else, huh?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, Ray, I said, he said, what would you do to them?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I said, Ray, if they ever do, they want to kill me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why? What would you do?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I said, I'd go home quick as I got able and get me an equalizer and go back and hunt them up. I said, I'd just eat myself up with them, that's something. And I never did jump on to them. I don't know why. I suppose he went and told them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Waylay, as Emmett, you met ups or no of them down here?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: No, I don't know.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Emmett was... Oh, Emmett, I know him. He was working at the mill. And he waylaid him and got him to come out and he had a board with a nail in the end of it. And what a fightin' he was at. And they'd Emmett up for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd they do that for?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Oh, because he doesn't have any better sense. He didn't have any, no way any better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why'd they pick on Emmett? Oh, probably something that happened at school, see?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He didn't lie.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: That's where he didn't do it. Here is another thing that this particular Sam Crumley did. He had this boy, uh...

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Harold.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Harold really not too bad a kid, for that matter, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know how kids'll fight coming out from school?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: They had to come by Crumley's and this boy and... And Harold got into a fight. And just not very far below Crumley's. So Sam went down there and the boys were down on the road just a -flying at her, which is kids.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: And, uh... The kid had Harold down on top of him and was just pounding him good. Harold was trying to pound back and fight back and so Sam just stepped in and... He had Harold on top of him. It wasn't very long until the kid was back on top and he'd done it three times. And then Pintner got killed that time, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who got killed? Almost?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Sam Pintner. Well, the kid had done it and lived right up.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: He had a brother that was there and he wore glasses. And he said to him the third time he'd done it, he said, Well, you know, that isn't right. He said, You're not doing right.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: So Sam... Walked off and turned around and slapped him in the face and broke his glasses. So they got the kids away and come home and... His dad was pretty hot -headed. I can't remember what his name was. They didn't live there, only just two winners. I can't remember, but his brother -in -law was Butts. And their kids come home, too. Up where Cochran's live now. Up where Butts is lived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did he do?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Butts' kids come home and told him about it. This kid went home and his nose was all bleeding and his glasses was broken. He told his dad what was the matter and he just went in the house and got his shotgun and took off down the road. And these guys got in a car and caught up with him. And walked him up and down the road and talked and talked and said, You can't do that. Said, Come on, let's just get in this car and we'll go down and have him. And when the girls was too young to have boyfriends, he'd all take them out to dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You really think he would have killed them if they hadn't stopped him?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: I think he would have killed him, yes. That's just what he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Sam's son that was in the fight?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, one of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the man that he hit was sort of a young fella?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: About the same. Yeah, he was about 14.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Same age?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: No, the boy that he hit. He was about 14 that Sam hit. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever give your sister trouble anymore after that?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, they never got along very good after that. She was kind of like I was. I don't know if she held a grudge, but she just was done with him.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I mean... She was through.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: But I agree he isn't that way anymore. Of course, she was married to him, and when you're married to a person, I think in that case, whenever he didn't do what she wanted to, he...

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: She done what he said. That's what he said. She done what he said. That's right. That's a better way to get out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you say?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He said what I said to him was very true.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you say to him?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Heh heh. I was driving a pair of young horses along to my stepfather. I had a hole in a bridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A hole in what? In a bridge?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: In a carver. And I didn't want to go close to the carver, and I didn't want to hurt him, because I was ready to hurt one of them or something. So I told him he'd come and grab the bitch, you know, and I told him you'd better take them fingers off of them bits. Well, they said I can see better than you can. I said you son of a gun, you better take them fingers off of them bits or I'll shoot them all. I pulled my gun. He got... he left her right quick and took to the house and run. Before he got to the house, he fell down three times and crawled apart at the time, but he went to the house right now. I'm just like the river guy. I figured I'd just as well do it now at any time.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: I saw that. Art used to get around pretty good, you know.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Sure. Sure. Hey, old one, well, I'll tell you, that happens in all neighborhoods.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: I think every neighborhood's got one. We got one up here now, the son of a gun.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, you think other people felt the same way pretty much about this son. He just wasn't much good as far as it is. No, he's... My stepfather was his own... Without all the friends he had left.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Ha ha ha ha ha.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't know how you like it.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: But, you know, I'm surprised about... I like Emmett Ott. I think he's a real good man. Emmett Ott had it.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Yeah, but Sam could have killed him with that board with a nail in it. My gosh. He got a hold of it and jerked it out of his hands and saw the nail and it was a mess for a while, but... At that time, why, Emmett's folks was living on that road where Frank lives now, way to the top of the hill. And Emmett was working in the mill.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: No, Emmett was going... Yeah, I guess he was going there. He was always going to school, but I don't know where.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: But it did fix him long ago, pal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Emmett is in pretty bad shape, isn't he?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, he wrote carriages too long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why, what does that do to you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, it just tires you all to pieces. That's all. You know, it's up and back just about as I can go. Them carriages, you know, and them big mills. Now, I tell them I may travel just this way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Back and forth, huh?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Just like that, just stopping like that. Well, it's got to tear you up. His half -brother rode a carriage there in Potlatch and then when they built the mill at Lewiston, they took him down there to break the riders in. It's a faster mill than this one in Potlatch. And Sam's half -brother.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Sam's half -brother, not Emmett's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sam?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah. And so he went down there and he broke six riders in down there. Showed them how and rode with them and showed them. Well, he died here quite a while ago, but he's humped back and everything and just... Chuck all to pieces. You know, man can only stand so much of that. It's like riding a bucking horse. You know, you can't stand... these buffaroos don't stand up under that very long. It just shakes and jars them up. Well, listen, did Emmett... did Emmett... you say he was pretty hot -headed, which I can see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get in much action when he was a youngster?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, no, he didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Emmett was awful hot -headed, but I never did know him to have any trouble, did you, buddy?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: No, I haven't known him to have any trouble. Never did. He seemed to have some pretty strong ideas about things. Oh, that was it. That's what I say. He was hot -headed. And, you know, somebody didn't come up with his idea. Well, you tell them about it right now, well, everybody knows him and been raised here and everybody knows him. They just walk off and leave and go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Didn't want no trouble. A fellow like that, that's the best way to do them. That makes them madder sometimes, it's time to talk to them. I would think that he was... some of the things he thought might have been pretty unpopular with some people. Yeah, he was over hot -headed. Well, I never had a run -in with him but once, and he bought a place from Earl Patton's.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: And he had a pretty fair house and a wonderful chicken house, and it was on skids. And we were thinking of building a chicken house, because now this had been years and years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I went down and asked Emmett, could we buy that chicken house?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Because they had a real good chicken house at that time.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Nope.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: He wouldn't sell it to me. He might need it sometime. And he let it sit right there and rot clear into the ground.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, it's his, I guess he could do as he pleased with it. I think Herschel said to me that he thought something, I don't know which war it was, it was World War I or World War II, but one of those wars, Emmett was really, couldn't see it at all. And he and Herschel didn't agree on that at all. Oh, no, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember if that was one or two?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't know. I don't know. Herschel was more World War I.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: And I don't remember now which one it was. Must have been one. Because Herschel, he went, I didn't get to, I couldn't, didn't pass examination, and I'd have went. So.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: What about, you told me one time about, you started telling me a story once about stealing chickens. I thought it was pretty funny. Let me tell that. Well, you told it pretty good. Maybe you both did. I didn't steal any chickens. I mean, you were, you know, that one. You and some other guys. Well, Charlie Taulkins down there, he probably knows him well. He's quite a b -s -er. Well, he'd get spelt stealing chickens. For just fun and a feat. They'd all get them a bottle or two of whiskey and go get them a three or four ole' hen someplace, you know, and have a big feed. Drink a few whiskey and spend the rest of the night together. Well. Cook the chickens and eat them tough as they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tough?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, and that was tough. And I was having a cook -off. They got in a hurry and started eating while they got gone. But anyhow, Charlie Taulkins, he lived there, where the post office is in Princeton, same building. He moved in that. So he moved the henhouse up within close, these bedroom windows, that door. And the two doors, the door and the window are right there together.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He's going around about one o 'clock. I got to say, I see some of them get hit with chickens, and he told the layout, you know. Well. Ralph Gordon and me is standing there, and we heard him, and that's all it took. So that night, Ralph and I went in, got six ole' hens, pulled the heads off. And left them right in the door so he'd find them the next morning, open the door. Nothing they never said about it. Oh, for, I don't know, it must have been 20 years, I guess. He's staying here with them one window here in Holland Wood for a minute. He said, yeah. That town about how you steal chickens, he'd tell me, I knowed how he done it. He'd tell me all about it, blowing around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I let him talk quite a while, and I said, did you ever find about five or six heads right in your kitchen door when you built your henhouse across the house there? Right by your bedroom window? Yeah, I said, you're the son of a bitch, don't you think?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, I wasn't alone when Ralph Gordon was with me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, were I? So that was a sport, huh, running around stealing chickens?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Well, we didn't do it for what was in it just, well, lots of times a girl would be in with us.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Tell him about the dance, the time that there an oafy, Sam's brother got off and walked the rest of the way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Well, you know when you had that dance up there and they broke up the meeting at the hall in Princeton, oafy said he was shot and somebody...

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh, that was pretty good. A lot of them wanted to go chicken -taking with her, they knew who it was and they were doing it, and some of the younger lads wanted to go with her. We took about 28 of them out one night and Moore in Moscow, he was running for prosecuting attorney, giving a big speech there in the house at Princeton. So we took them up to Commodore Hallows and we sent three plumbers on ahead of us to be there and be ready for us. They all had guns. Boy, we went to open that inhouse door and they cut loose on us. Well, there was two of them that had a lot of buckshot. Every time we shoot they'd throw buckshot right out into us, you know. Well, we took down the track and his half -brother was... we'd go on down the track. So Herschel had a nephew and had the old cripple from there. He hopped on the old mare and took down for Princeton and run right into the meeting where this fella was lecturing for prosecuting attorney and oafy be able to get shot, oafy be able to get shot. Well, that's his half -brother. So here they come, right up looking for us, you know. Oafy hunting them. So part of them, they didn't know what to think about it, so they get scared and they run on down. So we heard them coming back, so I got his half -brother on my back and I was packing him. So we went on and we talked on it. Here comes Joey Russell. He was quite a... thought he was a pretty husky man. We walked along there and talked to him a little bit and oafy he'd gotten and gone. He said, Dick, he said, I'm huskier than you are. He said, let me pack him. I said, here he is, he's getting pretty heavy. He packed him, I was putting him here at the road and he said, we're getting pretty close to him. You see, the lanterns are coming in on him. We're getting pretty close to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oafy said to Joey, he said, hey, Joe, and what?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: He said, if you just put me down, we can both bank better time on our feet. Let's get the hell out of here.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Oh boy, we had more fun.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: We'd all been into something, a bunch of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was just a joke, huh?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Turn your rig off and I'll tell you something that happened just recently. Go ahead. Well, um, Becker's lived right up here where Carpenters live now. And their oldest boy, name was, uh, Allen. And he married a gage girl, like Bernard's sister. And they come back down here and they were down to, they shivried him and they come down, they had him all made up with us. And, uh, they had gotten down there and they said, well, we'll go up to Dick May's and said, May don't feel very good and so they're not coming. And said, uh, the coyotes been getting their chickens. Travel a bit of both. We just had three old hems and they were about four years old. And, uh, so they said, we'll go up there and get those chickens. Said, they will know that we got them. Said, because the coyotes been getting them awful bad anyhow. So they come a -slipping up the road, you know, and Dick was out here. We used to have a cellar that sat right out here behind the house. Grandma Benge was here and Peggy and Johnny, they were old enough to know, they was all standing there, you know, just all ears and we could hear the boys coming up over there. And they'd be like, see ya. See a cigarette light every once in a while. Everything was just dark and quiet. And they got in the chicken house and the laughing and the hurting ones would say, well, there isn't but three here and they look awful scraggly. And they got the chickens and about one of them let us walk out of it. And Dick was out here at the corner of the house with a double barrel shotgun. No, that's the old 30 -30.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or was it the old 30 -30 shotgun?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: It made quite a racket that night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anyhow, what's going on up there?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: And he fired up in the air, you know, and Helen was so scared. Quite an emotional boy. He jumped down out of that and took off down there and fell down and tore his new wedding britches.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Erwin Menden. Now they run the store up there now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See that?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: She's Dick's niece. And he was just trying to keep up with him the best. And Lewis Tribble and Rex Tribble, that was her brothers, they was right in it and they all had their pockets full of shot. And Dick just kept shooting, you know. And every time he'd shoot, they'd throw buckshot at Alan. And he fell down. And again, he was a mess by the time. And Erwin got as far as the bridge and he just had to quit and lay down and just rolled and laugh. And Helen came in with his hair and scared to death and falling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he went, oh, what's the matter, Erwin? What happened?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: And Dick was shooting at her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, why have you shot a buckshot all over everything?

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: We'll look and see if we can find any blood on him. And everybody was, and you know, she got mad and went home and wouldn't speak to anybody.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: She just, oh, she was mad. She wouldn't speak to Erwin for a year.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Oh, boy. I wonder if he, I'd just like to ask Alan if he, if he remembered, I have him sewing for, oh, they're way down on the coast. She came and had lunch with me one time when the kids were small.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that stuff pretty normal for a chivalry?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: You do some...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's putting it off?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah, kind of was around here for years. What was some of the, what was, tell me some more about it. Because that, you know, they don't have that in a lot of places, the chivalry stuff. There are some places that people do that. I'll tell you one thing.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Peggy and Herman were married and it was before Keith was born. And Judy and Garfield lived in a little hog house that East Olmsted had built. Up there just, oh, a little ways from where Peggy and Herman lived. And so they moved up there. Real nice little log cabin.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: And they had four different chivalry crowds come, but Peggy and Herman hadn't been in on any of them. So they went one night, went out once, one night went someplace. And while they were gone, Wyherman crawled up on the roof and stuck a gunny sack in the end of the stovepipe. They got up the next morning, Garfield got up and built the fire. And the stove just went to smoking and Judy got up and she tried to do something. She opened drafts. Garfield got up and hit the pipe this way and that way. And it fell down all over the place. And he was dancing around there in his shorts trying to put them back together and burning his hands. And Judy went back to bed.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: That was what broke the case. Everyone got mad. Everyone had to put on his clothes. The stove was kind of hot so she greased the top of the stove and threw a couple of eggs on it. And he wrapped them between a sandwich and took off. And Judy went over to Peggy's and Herman's and she said, Well, all the dirty tricks, I climbed up there and found that they're gunny sacks stuffed in the chimney. Peggy laughed. It was the year before Peggy even told Judy that she was the one that done it. Oh, they were mad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you supposed to shivaree right after they got married? Was it supposed to be the same night or the same week or did it matter?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: It doesn't matter. Just when I get up with them sometimes. It might be the same night they got married. But the best one I think that I've ever had. Well, I like Herschel and his brother with Batschuh. Well, Herschel and I neither one was of age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you remember Kelly LaSalle?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Yeah. Well, he run around. He was about 16 I guess, 17. The old man couldn't even step to him. The old fella couldn't keep him to home. And he wouldn't do much. He'd come up there to trebles. He got there quite a bit. So we decided we'd get rid of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Guy Lamb was there and what else was there?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I believe Two Feathers was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: Urban Harris. Urban Harris and Two Feathers. That's what we called him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know him? No, why'd they call him Two Feathers?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: I don't know where he ever got that name at. That's what we always called him. And his elder, Herschel's brother. So Kelly said he had to go home. Well, alright. So we got to talking and somebody had seen a bear down around the treble. That's how it got started and that's what led to. So Herschel, he said, yeah, there's a bear been through here, a turmature, and he said to Kelly, he said, he'd been running up and down that draw here. Well, just out there by Herschel's barn, that was all timber.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: The barn isn't there anymore. Yeah, well the barn is right there, yes.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: But that's where the timber is, where Herschel lives.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: The barn, that's all timber. Yeah, but it wasn't where the barn is now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You wouldn't hear, would you?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: That was all timber in there then.

speaker: Ella Mae Benge
words: Yeah, but the barn isn't where, oh I see it was all timber where the barn is now. That's what I said, yeah.

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: So Herschel, he was walking along with Kelly. Of course we strung along the road. Well, got over to this bridge and, oh, Guy Lamb and I, you know, oh, it was with me, we come out here to growling, walked out right in front of them growling, you know, throwing our arms. It was pretty dark, but it was light enough they could see, you know. Kelly, he turned around and started to run, he got up on top of the hill and, so, well, that's all right. That's fine. So Guy Lamb and I went up on the hill and they stopped up there and was talking all over them. We sneaked right up by them and we was close to them, that pick up. And Herschel was standing there and he had a gun. He said, if it is somebody, they'd better say something, he said. I kept him, he said, I'll shoot. And Guy Lamb got scared. That scared guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Guy, you want to give yourself up?

speaker: John (Dick) Benge or Art Farley
words: And I said, oh no, I'm not. Stay quiet, stay quiet. I've been out with Herschel before, he knows what he's doing. I said, well, Kelly wanted the rifle. And Herschel said, no, I'm better shot than you are. I said, I'm used to gun, now I just keep it. And, of course, Guy would have known by that that he wasn't getting ready to hold the gun.
